scenarioName: Pneumonia Presentation — Adult with Early Sepsis

scenarioStory.history: 68-year-old female with a 3-day history of progressive illness that started as fatigue and a dry cough she initially attributed to seasonal allergies. The cough became productive over the second day, with thick yellow-green sputum, and was accompanied by chills, body aches, and a low-grade fever measured at home (101.2°F yesterday evening). Overnight she became more short of breath, with a sharp right-sided chest discomfort that worsens on deep inspiration and coughing. This morning her daughter found her weak, flushed, and a bit confused about what day it was — though she rapidly oriented after a few minutes of conversation. She denies hemoptysis, leg swelling, recent travel, sick contacts at home, vomiting, or diarrhea. She had a flu shot in October. No prior pneumonias. No prior intubation or ICU stay. She normally walks her dog 3 blocks daily without dyspnea.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS arrives to find a 68-year-old female with three days of productive cough, fever, weakness, and now mild dyspnea. She is alert and oriented x3 after a brief warm-up, speaking in mostly full sentences with occasional breath-catching pauses. She is visibly flushed, mildly diaphoretic, with a wet productive cough during the exam. Vital signs reveal a constellation that should prompt sepsis screening: temp 102.4°F, HR 112, RR 24, BP 102/64 (borderline), SpO2 91% room air. Right basilar crackles are present on focused auscultation. The teaching window of this Tier 1 scenario is: do a complete assessment, recognize this is not 'just pneumonia' — it is pneumonia plus early sepsis criteria (fever + tachycardia + tachypnea + suspected infection source) — and treat the whole patient (oxygen titration, IV access, fluid bolus, sepsis alert) rather than only the cough. → Full assessment performed: Crew has completed a full assessment and recognized both the pneumonia picture (focal RLL crackles, productive yellow-green sputum, fever, pleuritic chest pain) AND the early sepsis pattern (fever + HR 112 + RR 24 + suspected infection source + borderline BP 102/64 + brief AMS earlier). They now begin protocol-driven treatment: titrate oxygen to 94-98% (not 100%), establish IV access, start a 500 mL NS fluid bolus for the borderline BP / early septic shock, place the patient supine-tilted-comfortable, and prepare for a sepsis alert to the receiving ED. Capnography continues to read low-normal EtCO2 consistent with tachypnea/early perfusion stress. → Reassessed; trending toward goal: Post-bolus reassessment is documented and the patient is trending in the right direction. HR is drifting down (108 → 102), BP is up slightly into safer territory (112/68, MAP ~83), RR is 20, SpO2 is 95% on 3 L NC, EtCO2 waveform is normal at 34, and the patient is fully alert and oriented x3 with no further confusion. Focal RLL crackles persist (the pneumonia is still there — fluid and O2 don't fix pneumonia, antibiotics do). Crew transports semi-upright with continued reassessment q5min, has called a sepsis alert ahead, and gives a clean bedside handoff with full timeline, vitals trend, treatments, and infection source.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Three days of productive cough, fever, and progressive weakness; feeling 'really sick' since this morning.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS arrives to find a 68-year-old female with three days of productive cough, fever, weakness, and now mild dyspnea. She is alert and oriented x3 after a brief warm-up, speaking in mostly full sentences with occasional breath-catching pauses. She is visibly flushed, mildly diaphoretic, with a wet productive cough during the exam. Vital signs reveal a constellation that should prompt sepsis screening: temp 102.4°F, HR 112, RR 24, BP 102/64 (borderline), SpO2 91% room air. Right basilar crackles are present on focused auscultation. The teaching window of this Tier 1 scenario is: do a complete assessment, recognize this is not 'just pneumonia' — it is pneumonia plus early sepsis criteria (fever + tachycardia + tachypnea + suspected infection source) — and treat the whole patient (oxygen titration, IV access, fluid bolus, sepsis alert) rather than only the cough.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey (ABC); recognize respiratory distress with suspected pulmonary infection
Targeted history: onset, sputum, fever, sick contacts, prior steroids, hospitalizations
Full vital signs including TEMPERATURE and SpO2; note 102.4°F, HR 112, RR 24, BP 102/64, SpO2 91%
Auscultate all lung fields (anterior + posterior, upper/middle/lower); identify focal RLL crackles
Recognize early sepsis: suspected infection + ≥2 of fever, HR>100, RR>20, AMS, hypotension
Apply continuous SpO2 and waveform capnography; cardiac monitor; document baseline
Check blood glucose (result 138 mg/dL — no correction needed)

scenarioEvents.name: Full assessment performed
scenarioEvents.description: Crew has completed a full assessment and recognized both the pneumonia picture (focal RLL crackles, productive yellow-green sputum, fever, pleuritic chest pain) AND the early sepsis pattern (fever + HR 112 + RR 24 + suspected infection source + borderline BP 102/64 + brief AMS earlier). They now begin protocol-driven treatment: titrate oxygen to 94-98% (not 100%), establish IV access, start a 500 mL NS fluid bolus for the borderline BP / early septic shock, place the patient supine-tilted-comfortable, and prepare for a sepsis alert to the receiving ED. Capnography continues to read low-normal EtCO2 consistent with tachypnea/early perfusion stress.
relatedChecklist: Supplemental O2 by nasal cannula titrated to SpO2 94-98% (NOT 100%); escalate as needed
Establish IV access (18 ga preferred); if 2 failed attempts/<90 sec, place IO
NS fluid bolus 500 mL IV pressure-infused <15 min (70 kg × 30 mL/kg = 2100 mL goal; reassess after each 500 mL)
Position patient semi-upright (Fowler's/position of comfort) — supports respiratory mechanics
Advance notification to receiving ED — call sepsis alert with vitals and infection source
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO/PR for fever (70 kg × 15 mg/kg = 1050 mg → cap at 1000 mg max)

scenarioEvents.name: Reassessed; trending toward goal
scenarioEvents.description: Post-bolus reassessment is documented and the patient is trending in the right direction. HR is drifting down (108 → 102), BP is up slightly into safer territory (112/68, MAP ~83), RR is 20, SpO2 is 95% on 3 L NC, EtCO2 waveform is normal at 34, and the patient is fully alert and oriented x3 with no further confusion. Focal RLL crackles persist (the pneumonia is still there — fluid and O2 don't fix pneumonia, antibiotics do). Crew transports semi-upright with continued reassessment q5min, has called a sepsis alert ahead, and gives a clean bedside handoff with full timeline, vitals trend, treatments, and infection source.
relatedChecklist: Reassess full vitals, mental status, lung sounds after bolus and O2; document trend
Continuous SpO2, EtCO2, cardiac monitor en route; serial BP q5min; watch for re-deterioration
Repeat 500 mL NS bolus if SBP <100 or MAP <65 (total goal up to 30 mL/kg = ~2100 mL)
Transport to ED with sepsis alert; bedside handoff with timeline, vitals trend, treatments, source

scenarioEvents.name: Pneumonia-only; sepsis missed
scenarioEvents.description: Crew anchored on 'pneumonia' from the productive cough, fever, and crackles, applied a non-rebreather at 15 L/min to push the SpO2 to 100%, did not check or did not act on the temperature/HR/RR/BP pattern as sepsis criteria, did not establish IV access, and did not give a fluid bolus. They are transporting routinely without a sepsis alert. En route, the borderline BP has dropped (now 86/52), the patient is more fatigued and intermittently confused, and SpO2 reads 99% on the NRB — masking the perfusion problem. The teaching point: the diagnosis is pneumonia AND early sepsis. Treating only the cough/oxygenation while ignoring the perfusion picture lets compensated shock decompensate.
relatedChecklist: Recognize uncompensated shock: SBP 86, HR 124, RR 26, EtCO2 26, new AMS — distributive/septic
Establish IV/IO now; pressure-infuse 500 mL NS bolus (70 kg × 30 mL/kg = 2100 mL goal)
Titrate O2 down to nasal cannula 2-4 L; target SpO2 94-98%, NOT 100%
Call sepsis alert NOW; advance notification with vitals trend, lactate concern, infection source
If shock unresponsive to fluids → norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min (3.5–35 mcg/min for 70 kg)
